iti53:1.1: This was said by the Blessed One, said by the Arahant, so I have heard:
iti53:2.3: A feeling of pleasure, a feeling of pain, a feeling of neither pleasure nor pain.
iti53:2.4: A feeling of pleasure should be seen as stressful.
iti53:2.5: A feeling of pain should be seen as an arrow.
iti53:2.6: A feeling of neither pleasure nor pain should be seen as inconstant.
iti53:2.7:
When a monk has seen a feeling of pleasure as stressful, a feeling of pain as an arrow,
and a feeling of neither pleasure nor pain as inconstant, then he is called a monk who is noble,
iti53:2.8:
who has seen rightly, who has cut off craving, destroyed the fetters, and who–
from the right breaking-through of conceit–has put an end to suffering & stress.”
iti53:3.3: sees peaceful neither pleasure nor pain
iti53:4.1: he is a monk who’s seen rightly.
iti53:4.2: From that he is there released.
iti53:4.3: A master of direct knowing, at peace,
iti53:4.4: he is a sage gone beyond bonds.
